caption:
Guest of the «Made in Italy» column - @silviasalis 

Author: @iorestocosiofficial 

#mynewyork_ #news #ny #usa #magazine
text: Made in Italy by Debora Scalzo SILVIA SALIS The Courage of Leadership. The Voice of a Woman Guiding Genoa From Olympic Athlete to Mayor of Genoa, Silvia Salis shares her insights on leadership, courage, Italy looking toward the world. Silvia Salis embodies a new vision of Italian leadership. A former Olympic athlete, sports executive, and now Mayor of Genoa, her story spans discipline, determination, and vision combining the rigor of sport with the empathy of politics. MY NEW YORK magazine and the vision of a new As the first woman to lead a complex and symbolic city like Genoa in this historic moment, Salis represents a generation of women who don't ask for space they build it. Her journey speaks of deep roots, sacrifice, civic commitment, and a modern approach to 29 to public administration, capable of engaging with Europe and the world. For My New York Magazine, in the Made in Italy column, Silvia Salis shares her personal and institutional journey, the present of Genoa, and the future she envisions as a woman, a Mayor, and a global citizen. Mayor Salis, your journey is unique: from Olympic athlete to leading one of Europe’s most complex and fascinating cities. How has sports shaped the woman and political leader you are today? | was born into the world of sports my father was the caretaker of the athletics field at Villa Gentile in Genoa, and | started training at a very young age. It shaped my character. Sports change the way you approach life: when you're young, you want everything immediately, but preparing for competitions and the Olympics means planning years in advance, training, and working hard every single day with a long-term perspective. It's exactly the same mindset you need to govern a city: you have to manage the everyday while also planning complex projects that take years to come to fruition. | see many parallels between the world of sports and that of politics. You grew up ina humble family, deeply connected to work and community. How do your Genoese roots and personal history influence the way you govern the city? My life is tied to Genoa. As | mentioned, my father was the caretaker of the field where | grew up, while my mother worked for the city, first in Social Services and later in the municipal offices. At home, | learned the importance of working within the community. For many years | stayed rooted in Genoa, but, much to my dismay, | eventually had to move to other cities to train. It was difficult and painful | wanted to stay here. Even for this reason, today | feel a strong desire to give back to my city everything it gave me when | was younger. After a career at the highest levels of sport, vou chose to commit to public service, eventually entering politics. Was there a specific moment when you realized Genoa needed you to step forward? Yes, immediately, when | was asked. | realized that a civic figure like myself an Olympian and sports executive could unlock the situation within the center- left coalition regarding the choice of the mayoral candidate and help bring Genoa back into the progressive sphere. That's why | accepted. You are a woman who has often held roles traditionally dominated by men, without ever giving up your identity. How challenging has it been to assert an authoritative and credible female leadership in Italy? It's a challenge women face every day, but it's also an opportunity. Italy offers great possibilities, yet it still carries a strong legacy of low female representation at the highest levels of institutions. | believe it's not about imitating male models of power, but about forging a new path of leadership while staying true to your own qualities. Genoa is a city of the sea, of work, of migration, and of stil-open wounds but also a city of enormous potential. What is the first tangible transformation you want to leave as amark of your mandate? After years of center-right administration, the city needed to move from slogans to action. For example, we found the metro construction sites stalled, and Genoa hasn't inaugurated a new station since 2012. Our goal is to provide the city with a more extensive metro line, with connections that truly meet the needs of residents and tourists. In addition, we have an airport that needs to be fully functional and provide services aligned with the market, avoiding the excessively high prices we see today. Transportation is a key lever for economic development: we want to attract new businesses, but investments only come where services work, starting with mobility. Your platform emphasizes inclusion, rights, and attention to people. What is a female mayor's vision of social justice and the city in 2026? The vision is of a city with a social infrastructure capable of meeting the needs of all its residents, while respecting every individual's life choices. This is the challenge for a progressive city; to show that economic development and social justice can go hand in hand, without one undermining the other. We are working toward a city that can move quickly, but never leaves anyone behind. You dedicated vour election to vour father, a central figure in vour life. How important are emotion and empathy in your role as mayor? Emotion and empathy are fundamental to the way | approach politics. People need to know who is governing them, and it's essential to build a relationship of trust through an authentic image, not a constructed one. It means staying true to yourself, not chasing the emotional waves of the moment, and maintaining an institutional stance without losing empathy. It's a quality | believe truly defines me. Genoa is regaining a strong voice beyond national borders. What role do you envision for your city in the European and international dialogue, in terms of culture, innovation, and sustainability? Genoa is a maritime city, a port, historically open to the world, and it must continue along this path, which brings opportunities and introduces our territory to those who may eventually choose to live here. We need young people and families investing in their future here, finding concrete answers to their needs. That's why it's essential to focus on innovation, sustainability, and an attractive cultural program: these are key elements to ensure that people choose Genoa and choose to stay. Alongside the quality of services, a social infrastructure is necessary so that the burden of daily care does not fall solely on families. 30 New York is a symbol of energy, renewal, and female leadership. What is your relationship with the United States, and what message do you want to send, as an Italian mayor, to the American readers of My New York Magazine? | have visited the United States many times, a country | never tire of exploring. | particularly love New York I've been there at least ten times. I'm drawn to its energy, its magnetism, its cultural output, and its extraordinary multiculturalism. It makes you feel at the center of the world. Today, it is experiencing a significant progressive wave under Mayor Zohran Mamdani, an internationally influential leader. He is a political figure who gives hope: from a city like New York, a new model of politics can emerge, one that is close to the people and far from the populist and nationalist drift the U.S. has taken which, in my view, does not represent the country. | believe Mamdani is today the foremost example of an administrative model that prioritizes inclusion and social policies, in a city historically devoted to business and enterprise, but one that must never lose sight of its humanity. Looking ahead, what are the most important projects awaiting you in the coming years? And if you look at yourself not only as mayor but as a woman, where do you see yourself at the end of this journey? The most important projects concern transportation, but | am also deeply committed to a new social and urban redevelopment plan. There is a major challenge I'm working on, and | hope to be able to share it soon with the city. On a personal level, | believe journeys like this never truly end: life is a series of moments, opportunities, and commitments. It's not important where | will be at the end of these five years, but | hope Genoa will benefit from a series of concrete improvements. My focus today is entirely on this living, beautiful entity that is the city of Genoa. magazine
